title: The Upload That Worked Everywhere Else

personality.role: backend engineer, joined the debugging thread late
personality.demeanor: patient, has seen this before, states facts without judgment
personality.recurring_concern: upload feature is core product, law firm customers depending on it

facts: frontend developer Rina sent another Slack message confirming presigned URL is valid (tested with curl, attached output)
she is re-reading the IAM policy JSON for the third time
backend engineer Tomas joined the thread
API Gateway logs show presigned URL generated correctly
S3 bucket policy checked, everything looks fine from server side
Tomas asked if anyone has checked the browser console
IAM policy and presigned URL are both correct
the difference is in how the browser makes the request, not in the permissions

glossary.CORS: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing. A browser security feature that blocks web pages from making requests to a different domain unless the server explicitly allows it.
glossary.preflight request: An automatic OPTIONS request the browser sends before certain cross-origin requests (like PUT) to ask the server if the real request is allowed.
glossary.presigned URL: A temporary URL that includes authentication information in its query string, allowing anyone with the URL to perform a specific S3 operation without AWS credentials.
glossary.origin: A combination of protocol, domain, and port (e.g., https://app.pollen.io). Two URLs with different origins are considered cross-origin by the browser.
glossary.same-origin policy: A browser security rule that prevents JavaScript on one origin from reading responses from a different origin, unless the server opts in via CORS headers.
glossary.origin request policy: A CloudFront configuration that controls which headers, cookies, and query strings CloudFront forwards from the viewer to the origin server.
glossary.cache invalidation: A CloudFront operation that removes cached objects from edge locations before they expire, forcing CloudFront to fetch fresh copies from the origin.
glossary.OPTIONS method: An HTTP method used by browsers for preflight requests. It asks the server what methods and headers are allowed for cross-origin requests.

narrative_arc.call: alert arrives, stakes established from story.md Opening
narrative_arc.threshold: player enters investigation, upload works from non-browser clients but fails in browser, developer has been reading IAM policies for two hours
narrative_arc.trials: browser console shows CORS error, S3 bucket has no CORS configuration, after adding CORS config upload still fails because CloudFront strips Origin header before it reaches S3
narrative_arc.revelation: two-layer problem -- S3 needs CORS rules, CloudFront needs origin request policy forwarding Origin, Access-Control-Request-Headers, and Access-Control-Request-Method headers, both must be fixed together
narrative_arc.return: both changes applied, upload works, cache invalidation clears stale responses, team wiki updated with CORS-first debugging note

components.name: Browser at app.pollen.io
components.role: Sends preflight OPTIONS requests and enforces same-origin policy before cross-origin PUT uploads
components.failure_impact: If the browser receives no CORS headers, it blocks the upload silently with no user-facing error message

components.name: API Gateway at api.pollen.io
components.role: Generates presigned URLs via Lambda for browser-based document uploads to S3
components.failure_impact: If the API Gateway fails, the browser cannot obtain a presigned URL to upload documents

components.name: CloudFront distribution d-EDFDVBD6EXAMPLE
components.role: Caches responses and forwards requests to S3, but strips Origin header without a CORS origin request policy
components.failure_impact: If CloudFront strips CORS headers, S3 cannot respond to preflight requests and browser uploads fail

components.name: S3 bucket pollen-docs-prod
components.role: Stores uploaded documents and receives PUT requests via presigned URLs
components.failure_impact: Without CORS configuration, S3 returns 403 on OPTIONS preflight requests from browsers

system_narration.data_flow: Browser requests a presigned URL from API Gateway. Lambda generates the URL and returns it. Browser uses the URL to PUT a file to S3 through CloudFront. Before the PUT, the browser sends an OPTIONS preflight to CloudFront. CloudFront forwards the request to S3 but strips the Origin header. S3 has no CORS configuration and returns 403 on the OPTIONS. The browser blocks the PUT.
system_narration.what_broke: Two things broke. First, the S3 bucket had no CORS configuration, so it could not respond to preflight requests with the required Access-Control-Allow-Origin header. Second, CloudFront used the default CachingOptimized policy with no origin request policy, so it did not forward the Origin, Access-Control-Request-Headers, or Access-Control-Request-Method headers to S3. Even after fixing S3, CloudFront would cache non-CORS responses and serve them to browsers.

hints.hint: the upload works outside the browser -- what is different about browser HTTP requests?
check the browser developer tools Network tab and look at the request headers
browsers send preflight OPTIONS requests for cross-origin PUT requests
the S3 bucket has no CORS configuration -- run aws s3api get-bucket-cors
CloudFront is also stripping the Origin header -- check the cache behavior's origin request policy

resolution.root_cause: The S3 bucket pollen-docs-prod has no CORS configuration, causing browsers to block cross-origin PUT requests via presigned URLs. Additionally, the CloudFront distribution d-EDFDVBD6EXAMPLE is configured with a cache policy that does not forward the Origin, Access-Control-Request-Headers, or Access-Control-Request-Method headers to S3, so even after adding CORS config to S3, CloudFront would strip the headers needed for CORS negotiation.

fix_criteria.description: Player identifies that the S3 bucket has no CORS configuration, causing browser uploads to fail
Player identifies that CloudFront is not forwarding the Origin header to S3
Player proposes a correct CORS JSON configuration for the S3 bucket that allows PUT from the application origin
Player explains why curl and Postman work but browsers do not -- browsers enforce the same-origin policy and send preflight OPTIONS requests

learning_objectives: CORS is enforced by browsers, not servers -- non-browser clients like curl and Postman skip CORS entirely
S3 buckets require explicit CORS configuration to respond to cross-origin requests with the correct Access-Control headers
CloudFront must forward Origin, Access-Control-Request-Headers, and Access-Control-Request-Method headers for CORS to work end-to-end
Presigned URLs handle authentication and authorization but do not bypass CORS -- these are separate mechanisms
sop_steps: Confirm the pattern: the upload fails in the browser, but the exact same presigned URL works perfectly from non-browser tools like curl, Postman, or a Python script. This pattern almost always means the problem is CORS -- a browser security rule, not a permissions issue.

Open the browser's developer tools (usually F12). Check the Console tab for a CORS error message and the Network tab for a failed OPTIONS request. The OPTIONS request is a 'preflight check' -- the browser asks the server 'are you willing to accept this upload from my domain?' before sending the actual file.

Check whether the S3 bucket has CORS rules configured. Run aws s3api get-bucket-cors --bucket pollen-docs-prod. CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is a set of rules that tells the browser which websites are allowed to make requests to this bucket.

If the CORS rules are empty or missing, create a configuration file that allows your application's domain (https://app.pollen.io), the HTTP methods it needs (GET, PUT, POST, HEAD), any request headers (AllowedHeaders [*]), and headers the browser should be able to read from the response (ExposeHeaders [ETag, x-amz-request-id]).
Apply the CORS configuration to the bucket: aws s3api put-bucket-cors --bucket pollen-docs-prod --cors-configuration file://cors.json.
Test the browser upload again. If it still fails, the problem has a second layer -- CloudFront (the content delivery network sitting between the browser and S3) may be stripping important headers.

Check the CloudFront distribution's cache behavior settings. Look at whether an origin request policy is attached. The origin request policy controls which headers CloudFront forwards from the browser to S3. Without the right policy, CloudFront strips the Origin header, and S3 never sees that the request came from a browser, so it does not include CORS headers in the response.

If no origin request policy is set, attach the AWS-managed CORS-S3Origin policy (ID: 88a5eaf4-2fd4-4709-b370-b4c650ea3fcf). This tells CloudFront to forward the three headers needed for CORS: Origin, Access-Control-Request-Headers, and Access-Control-Request-Method.

Clear CloudFront's cache so stale responses without CORS headers are removed. Run aws cloudfront create-invalidation --distribution-id EDFDVBD6EXAMPLE --paths '/*'. Without this step, CloudFront may keep serving old cached responses that lack the CORS headers.
Verify the fix end-to-end: upload a file from the browser at https://app.pollen.io. Check the browser console for any remaining CORS errors and confirm the file appears in the S3 bucket.

Add a checklist item to the team's bucket creation process: whenever browser clients will access an S3 bucket directly, configure CORS rules and the CloudFront origin request policy before the feature ships.

related_failure_modes.scenario: CORS rules exist but the allowed website list does not include your application's domain
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The bucket has CORS rules, but the list of allowed origins (websites that are permitted to make requests) does not include the application's domain. S3 returns a specific error: 'This CORS request is not allowed.' The fix is adding the missing domain to the AllowedOrigins list, not creating the entire CORS configuration from scratch.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Use specific domain names in your AllowedOrigins list (not wildcards) so you always know which sites have access. Review the list whenever you add a new frontend domain.

related_failure_modes.scenario: CloudFront caches a response without CORS headers and serves it to browsers
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: CORS is correctly configured on S3. Direct browser requests to S3 work fine. But requests through CloudFront fail intermittently. This happens because CloudFront cached a response from a non-browser client (like a health check or curl request) that did not include CORS headers. When a browser request arrives next, CloudFront serves the cached response -- which is missing the CORS headers the browser needs.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Always attach an origin request policy that forwards the Origin header so CloudFront caches different responses for browser and non-browser requests separately.

related_failure_modes.scenario: The presigned URL's signature does not match because the browser added extra headers
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The error message says 'SignatureDoesNotMatch' instead of showing a CORS error. This happens when the browser adds headers (like Content-Type with a value different from what was expected) that were not accounted for when the presigned URL was created. The URL's built-in signature only covers the headers specified at generation time.
related_failure_modes.prevention: When generating a presigned URL, include all headers the browser will send -- especially Content-Type -- in the list of signed headers.

related_failure_modes.scenario: The bucket's access policy blocks the preflight check
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: CORS rules are configured on the bucket, but a separate access control document (called a bucket policy) explicitly denies all HTTP methods except GET and PUT. The browser's preflight check uses the OPTIONS method, which the bucket policy blocks before S3 even looks at the CORS rules.
related_failure_modes.prevention: When CORS is needed, make sure the bucket policy does not block the OPTIONS method. S3 must receive the OPTIONS request to return the CORS headers the browser needs.

sop_practices: Whenever a browser will upload files directly to S3, set up CORS rules on the bucket before the feature goes live. Test with a preflight check (curl -X OPTIONS) to confirm the rules are working before users see the feature.

When CloudFront (a content delivery network) sits between the browser and S3, attach the AWS-managed CORS-S3Origin origin request policy. This tells CloudFront to forward the browser headers that S3 needs to respond with CORS permissions. Without this policy, CloudFront strips those headers and S3 does not know the request came from a browser.

After changing CORS rules or CloudFront policies, clear the CloudFront cache (called a cache invalidation) for all paths (/*). Stale cached responses without CORS headers will otherwise keep being served to browsers until they expire naturally.
Add CORS verification to the team's deployment checklist for any feature where a browser talks directly to S3 -- including presigned URL uploads and static website hosting.
